summary: American architect and urban planner (1846–1912), associated with the City Beautiful movement. Director of Works for the 1893 World's Columbian Exposition in Chicago ('White City'). Designed the Flatiron Building in New York, Union Station in Washington D.C., and the 1909 Plan of Chicago (co-authored with Edward H. Bennett). Long partner with John Wellborn Root.

works.name: World's Columbian Exposition / White City (1893)
works.description: Burnham served as Director of Works for the 1893 World's Columbian Exposition in Chicago, which featured a complex of white Beaux-Arts buildings nicknamed the 'White City.' The event was held in Jackson Park and celebrated the 400th anniversary of Columbus's arrival in America. It featured the first Ferris Wheel and the debut lecture of Frederick Jackson Turner's Frontier Thesis. Mayor Carter Harrison was assassinated during the exposition. Frederick Law Olmsted designed a Wooded Island for it. The serial killer H.H. Holmes was active during it. Erik Larson chronicled it in The Devil in the White City. McKim, Mead & White and Augustus Saint-Gaudens also contributed. Louis Sullivan and Dankmar Adler designed the Transportation Building in deliberate opposition to the neoclassical style of the other buildings; Sullivan later argued the neoclassicism set American architecture back half a century. Edmonia Lewis's sculptures were exhibited at a Palace of Fine Arts envisioned as part of the White City. The fairgrounds are now near the Museum of Science and Industry. Wikipedia

works.name: Flatiron Building
works.description: Triangular skyscraper in New York City (1902), originally named the Fuller Building, located between Fifth Avenue, Broadway, and East 22nd Street. Designed with co-architect Frederick Dinkelberg. A classical Greek column inspired the narrow triangular floor plan. Stieglitz and Steichen photographed it. Wikipedia

works.name: Plan of Chicago (1909)
works.description: Burnham's 1909 City Beautiful urban plan for Chicago, co-authored with Edward H. Bennett and published by the city's Commercial Club. It created an unbroken stretch of parks along Lake Michigan through landfill and shaped Wacker Drive (a double-decker street) and Burnham Park. The plan also preserved a 'string of forests connecting several parks,' called the Emerald Necklace. Burnham Park still contains a monument to Italian air marshal Italo Balbo, who led a 1933 propaganda tour from Rome to Chicago. Wikipedia

works.name: Manila / Baguio Plan
works.description: William Howard Taft hired Burnham to redesign the city of Manila in the Philippines and plan the summer capital of Baguio in the tropical pine forests of Luzon. Hundreds of workers died building the Kennon Road connecting them. William E. Parsons later implemented the City Beautiful plan for Manila, which included the first Gabaldon schoolhouses. Daniel Immerwahr called him an 'architectural imperialist' who operated without local approval in How to Hide an Empire. Wikipedia

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Daniel Burnham (1846–1912) worked closely with partner John Wellborn Root; together they designed the Masonic Temple Building (featuring a glass rooftop dome and alpine corner roof sections, now demolished) and the Monadnock Building in Chicago. After the Great Fire of Chicago, Burnham designed the Montauk Block with fire shutters and a fireproof iron skeleton. He was a member of the McMillan Commission that redesigned the National Mall. He also designed Union Station in Washington D.C., the Union Stock Yard Gate in Chicago (a three-archway gatehouse with turrets and a terracotta bull's head), and the public library in Joliet. The Baths of Diocletian inspired a design featuring a three-arch loggia in a longer arcade.

comprehensive_summary:
Daniel Burnham (1846–1912) was an American architect and urban planner closely associated with the City Beautiful movement. He is best known as the Director of Works for the 1893 World's Columbian Exposition in Chicago, where his plan created a complex of white Beaux-Arts buildings nicknamed the 'White City.' The fairgrounds were located in Jackson Park and are now near the Museum of Science and Industry. The event debuted the first Ferris Wheel, hosted Frederick Jackson Turner's inaugural lecture on the Frontier Thesis, and was the setting for the H.H. Holmes murders chronicled by Erik Larson in The Devil in the White City. Mayor Carter Harrison was assassinated during the fair. McKim, Mead & White and Augustus Saint-Gaudens also contributed, while Louis Sullivan and Dankmar Adler deliberately designed their colorful Transportation Building in opposition to Burnham's Beaux-Arts style; Sullivan later argued that the 1893 fair's neoclassicism set American architecture back half a century. Edmonia Lewis's sculptures were displayed at a Palace of Fine Arts envisioned as part of the White City.

Burnham's most iconic single building is the Flatiron Building (1902) in New York City, originally named the Fuller Building, standing at the triangular intersection of Fifth Avenue, Broadway, and East 22nd Street. Co-designed with Frederick Dinkelberg, a classical Greek column inspired its narrow triangular floor plan. It was photographed in pictorialist compositions by Albert Stieglitz and Edward Steichen, and its ground-floor 'cow-catcher' retail space is said to have apocryphally inspired Edward Hopper's Nighthawks.

Burnham's 1909 Plan of Chicago—a City Beautiful initiative co-authored with Edward H. Bennett and published by the Commercial Club—created an unbroken stretch of parks along Lake Michigan through landfill and led to the construction of the double-decked Wacker Drive to reduce traffic. The plan also preserved a chain of forests connecting several parks, called the 'Emerald Necklace.' Burnham Park, established through the plan, still contains a monument to Italian fascist air marshal Italo Balbo, who led a 1933 propaganda flight from Rome to Chicago.

Hired by William Howard Taft, Burnham designed plans for Manila and for Baguio, the colonial summer capital in the pine forests of the Philippines. Hundreds of workers died building the Kennon Road connecting Manila to Baguio. William E. Parsons later implemented the City Beautiful plan for Manila, building the first Gabaldon schoolhouses. Daniel Immerwahr characterized Burnham as an 'architectural imperialist' in How to Hide an Empire. Burnham's motto—'make no little plans'—was said to have inspired George Ferris's design for the original Ferris Wheel.

Burnham worked in long partnership with John Wellborn Root. Together they designed the Monadnock Building and Masonic Temple Building (now demolished, with a glass rooftop dome and alpine corner roof sections) in Chicago. After the Great Fire of Chicago, he designed the Montauk Block with fire shutters and a fireproof iron skeleton. He served on the McMillan Commission that redesigned the National Mall and also designed Washington D.C.'s Union Station, Chicago's Union Stock Yard Gate (a three-archway gatehouse with a terracotta bull's head), and the public library in Joliet. A Baths of Diocletian–inspired design features a three-arch loggia in a longer arcade.
